questions.question: What are undefined terms in geometry? Name the three undefined terms in Euclid's geometry.
questions.answer: Undefined terms are basic geometric notions that cannot be defined without circularity. The three undefined terms are: point, line, and plane (plane surface).
questions.solution:
When Euclid tried to define a point as 'that which has no part', the word 'part' itself needs defining, and defining 'part' requires other undefined words — leading to an endless chain. To avoid this, mathematicians agree to leave certain fundamental terms undefined.

The three undefined terms in Euclid's geometry:
1. Point — represented as a dot; has no dimensions (no length, breadth, or thickness).
2. Line — has only one dimension (length); is breadthless.
3. Plane (surface) — has two dimensions (length and breadth only).

Though we cannot formally define these, we understand them intuitively and can represent them using physical models.

questions.question: Distinguish between an axiom and a theorem.
questions.answer: An axiom is an obvious universal truth accepted without proof. A theorem is a statement that is proved using definitions, axioms, and previously proved results by deductive reasoning.
questions.solution:
Axiom:
- A statement accepted as obviously true without requiring any proof.
- Forms the starting point of a logical system.
- Example: 'The whole is greater than the part.'

Theorem:
- A statement that must be justified through a logical proof.
- Proved using definitions, axioms, previously proved theorems, and deductive reasoning.
- Example: 'Two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in common.' (Theorem 5.1)

Key distinction: Axioms are assumed; theorems are proved. All theorems ultimately rest on axioms.

questions.question: Distinguish between an axiom and a postulate in Euclid's system.
questions.answer: Axioms (common notions) are assumptions that apply throughout all of mathematics. Postulates are assumptions specific to geometry. In modern usage, both terms are used interchangeably.
questions.solution:
In Euclid's system:
- Axioms (Common Notions): General truths applicable across all of mathematics, not limited to geometry. Example: 'Things equal to the same thing are equal to one another.'
- Postulates: Assumptions specific to geometry. Example: 'A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.'

Today, the distinction has been dropped and both terms 'axiom' and 'postulate' are used to mean the same thing — statements accepted without proof as the starting assumptions of a logical system.

questions.question: State Euclid's five postulates.
questions.answer: P1: A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other. P2: A terminated line can be produced indefinitely. P3: A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius. P4: All right angles are equal. P5: If a line crossing two lines makes interior angles on one side summing to less than 180°, those two lines meet on that side.
questions.solution:
Euclid's Five Postulates:

Postulate 1: A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.

Postulate 2: A terminated line (line segment) can be produced indefinitely in either direction (i.e., a line segment can be extended to form a full line).

Postulate 3: A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius.

Postulate 4: All right angles are equal to one another.

Postulate 5: If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles, the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side.

Note: Postulates 1–4 are simple and self-evident. Postulate 5 is significantly more complex — it is the famous parallel postulate.

questions.question: How many dimensions does a solid have? A surface? A line? A point?
questions.answer: Solid: 3 dimensions. Surface: 2 dimensions. Line: 1 dimension. Point: 0 dimensions (no dimensions).
questions.solution:
Euclid described geometry by thinking of how we get from solids to points, losing one dimension at each step:

- Solid: Has shape and size in 3 directions → 3 dimensions (length, breadth, height).
- Surface (boundary of a solid): Has length and breadth only → 2 dimensions.
- Line (boundary of a surface): Has length only → 1 dimension.
- Point (end of a line): Has no dimensions → 0 dimensions.

This progression (solid → surface → line → point) is the conceptual foundation for all of Euclidean geometry.

questions.question: State Euclid's first axiom and give one example of its application.
questions.answer: Axiom 1: Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another. Example: If AB = PQ and PQ = XY, then AB = XY.
questions.solution:
Euclid's first axiom states: 'Things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another.'

This is essentially the transitivity of equality: if a = b and b = c, then a = c.

Example:
Given AB = PQ and PQ = XY.
Since AB and XY are both equal to PQ (the same thing), by Axiom 1:
  AB = XY.

This axiom applies to all magnitudes — lengths, areas, angles, volumes — as long as they are of the same kind and can be meaningfully compared.

questions.question: Which of the following statements are true and which are false? (i) Only one line can pass through a single point. (ii) There are an infinite number of lines which pass through two distinct points. (iii) A terminated line can be produced indefinitely on both sides.
questions.answer: (i) False. (ii) False — only one unique line passes through two distinct points. (iii) True.
questions.solution:
(i) 'Only one line can pass through a single point.' → FALSE.
Infinitely many lines can pass through any single point. A single point does not uniquely determine a line.

(ii) 'There are infinite lines through two distinct points.' → FALSE.
By Axiom 5.1 (derived from Postulate 1): Given two distinct points, there is a unique line passing through them. Only ONE line passes through two distinct points.

(iii) 'A terminated line can be produced indefinitely on both sides.' → TRUE.
This follows directly from Euclid's Postulate 2: a terminated line (line segment) can be extended indefinitely. Since it can be extended from either end, it can be produced on both sides.

questions.question: State Euclid's fifth axiom. What does it define?
questions.answer: Axiom 5: The whole is greater than the part. It defines the meaning of 'greater than' — if B is a part of A, then A > B, meaning A = B + C for some positive quantity C.
questions.solution:
Euclid's Fifth Axiom: 'The whole is greater than the part.'

This axiom gives the definition of 'greater than' in terms of parts:
If quantity B is a part of quantity A, then A = B + C for some positive quantity C, and we say A > B.

Example: A line segment AC contains a shorter segment AB within it. The remaining part BC is positive, so AC = AB + BC, meaning AC > AB and AC > BC.

This axiom is used repeatedly in geometry when we argue that removing a part from a whole leaves a smaller positive remainder.

questions.question: What is a consistent system of axioms?
questions.answer: A system of axioms is called consistent if it is impossible to derive from those axioms any statement that contradicts another axiom or a previously proved statement.
questions.solution:
A consistent system of axioms is one where:
- No two axioms contradict each other.
- No theorem derived from the axioms contradicts any axiom or another theorem.

Why it matters: If a system is inconsistent, contradictory results can be proved — making the entire system unreliable for logical deduction. Before using any set of axioms to build mathematics or geometry, we must verify that the system is consistent.

Euclid's system was assumed to be consistent, though formal proofs of consistency came much later in the development of mathematics.

questions.question: What is Axiom 5.1 (as stated in the textbook)? How is it derived from Euclid's postulates?
questions.answer: Axiom 5.1: Given two distinct points, there is a unique line that passes through them. It is derived from Postulate 1 (which says a line can be drawn through two points, but does not guarantee uniqueness) — uniqueness was assumed by Euclid without explicit statement.
questions.solution:
Postulate 1 states: 'A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.' This tells us that a line exists between two distinct points but does not say there is only one such line.

However, Euclid consistently assumed (without explicitly stating) that only one line passes through two distinct points. The textbook formalises this as:

Axiom 5.1: Given two distinct points, there is a unique line that passes through them.

Verification: Given two points P and Q, the line PQ is the only line through both. Any other line through P cannot also pass through Q (it would have to coincide with PQ), confirming the uniqueness.

questions.question: If A, B, and C are three points on a line with B lying between A and C, prove that AB + BC = AC using Euclid's axioms.
questions.answer: By Axiom 4 (things that coincide are equal): AB + BC coincides with AC, so AB + BC = AC.
questions.solution:
Given: Points A, B, C are collinear with B between A and C.
To prove: AB + BC = AC.

Proof:
Step 1: Since B lies between A and C on the line, the segment AC is made up of segments AB and BC placed together end-to-end.

Step 2: Therefore, the combined length AB + BC coincides exactly with the length AC — they represent the same thing.

Step 3: By Euclid's Axiom 4: 'Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.'

Step 4: Since AB + BC coincides with AC:
  AB + BC = AC

(Note: This proof also implicitly uses the axiom that a unique line passes through two distinct points.)

questions.question: Prove Theorem 5.1: Two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in common.
questions.answer: Assume two lines meet at two points. Then two lines pass through two distinct points — contradicting Axiom 5.1 (unique line through two points). So two distinct lines can share at most one point.
questions.solution:
Given: Two distinct lines l and m.
To prove: They cannot have more than one point in common.

Proof (by contradiction):

Assume, for contradiction, that lines l and m intersect at two distinct points P and Q.

Then: Both line l and line m pass through the two distinct points P and Q.
So we have two different lines passing through the same two points P and Q.

But by Axiom 5.1: Given two distinct points, there is a unique line that passes through them.
This means only ONE line can pass through both P and Q — not two.

This contradicts our assumption that l and m are two different lines both passing through P and Q.

Conclusion: Our assumption was wrong. Therefore, two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in common.

questions.question: If AC = BD, prove that AB = CD. (Given that A, B, C, D are four distinct points on a line in that order).
questions.answer: AB = AC − BC and CD = BD − BC. Since AC = BD, subtracting BC (equals from equals), AB = CD.
questions.solution:
Given: Points A, B, C, D lie on a line in that order. AC = BD.
To prove: AB = CD.

Proof:
Since B lies between A and C:
  AC = AB + BC  → AB = AC − BC  … (1)

Since C lies between B and D:
  BD = BC + CD  → CD = BD − BC  … (2)

Given: AC = BD  … (3)

Substituting (3) into (1) and (2):
  AB = AC − BC
  CD = AC − BC   [since BD = AC]

By Euclid's Axiom 3: 'If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.'
Since equals (BC) are subtracted from equals (AC = BD):
  AB = CD  ✓

questions.question: A point C lies between two points A and B such that AC = BC. Prove that AC = ½ AB.
questions.answer: AC + BC = AB (C between A and B). Since AC = BC, we get 2AC = AB, so AC = ½AB.
questions.solution:
Given: Point C lies between A and B, and AC = BC.
To prove: AC = ½ AB.

Proof:
Since C lies between A and B:
  AC + CB = AB  (by coincidence of segments, Axiom 4)

Given: AC = BC, so CB = AC. Substituting:
  AC + AC = AB
  2AC = AB

By Euclid's Axiom 7: 'Things which are halves of the same things are equal to one another.'
Dividing both sides by 2:
  AC = ½ AB  ✓

This also shows that C is the midpoint of AB — the point that divides AB into two equal halves.

questions.question: Prove that every line segment has one and only one midpoint.
questions.answer: Assume two midpoints C and D exist on AB. Then AC = ½AB and AD = ½AB, so AC = AD, making C and D the same point — contradiction. Hence the midpoint is unique.
questions.solution:
Given: A line segment AB.
To prove: AB has one and only one midpoint.

Existence: There exists at least one midpoint (a point that divides AB into two equal halves) — we accept this geometrically.

Uniqueness (proof by contradiction):
Assume AB has two distinct midpoints C and D, with C closer to A.

Since C is the midpoint: AC = CB = ½ AB  … (1)
Since D is the midpoint: AD = DB = ½ AB  … (2)

From (1) and (2): AC = AD [both equal ½ AB]

But A, C, D are on segment AB with C between A and D (assumed C closer to A).
If AC = AD, then D coincides with C → C and D are the same point.

This contradicts our assumption that C and D are two distinct midpoints.

Conclusion: Every line segment has one and only one midpoint.

questions.question: Two salesmen make equal sales during a particular month. In the next month, each doubles his sales volume. Will their sales still be equal? Which Euclid's axiom justifies your answer?
questions.answer: Yes, their sales will still be equal. This is justified by Euclid's Axiom 6: 'Things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.'
questions.solution:
Given: Sales of Salesman 1 = Sales of Salesman 2 (say, both equal S) in month 1.
In month 2, both double their sales:
  Salesman 1's new sales = 2S
  Salesman 2's new sales = 2S

Since both are doubles of the same quantity S:
By Euclid's Axiom 6: 'Things which are double of the same things are equal to one another.'
  2S = 2S → Their sales are still equal.

Axiom 6 captures the principle that multiplying equals by the same factor gives equals — a fundamental rule of algebra rooted in Euclid's common notions.

questions.question: Consider the two postulates: (i) Given any two distinct points A and B, there exists a third point C which lies between A and B. (ii) There exist at least three points that are not on the same line. Do these postulates contain undefined terms? Are they consistent?
questions.answer: Yes, both postulates contain undefined terms (point, line, between). They are consistent — neither leads to a contradiction, and they are compatible with Euclid's postulates though not directly derivable from them.
questions.solution:
Analysis of the two postulates:

Undefined Terms:
- Postulate (i) uses: 'point' (undefined), 'between' (not formally defined by Euclid).
- Postulate (ii) uses: 'point' (undefined), 'line' (undefined), 'not on the same line' (collinearity — depends on undefined terms).
So yes, both contain undefined terms.

Consistency:
- Postulate (i): There always exists a point between two distinct points (e.g., the midpoint). This doesn't contradict any of Euclid's postulates or axioms.
- Postulate (ii): We can always find three non-collinear points in a plane; this is a basic geometric fact that doesn't contradict Euclid's system.
- Neither postulate contradicts the other; together they are mutually consistent.

Do they follow from Euclid's postulates?
Not directly — Euclid's five postulates do not explicitly mention these statements. They are additional assumptions (independent from but compatible with Euclid's system).

questions.question: Prove that an equilateral triangle can be constructed on any given line segment, using Euclid's postulates.
questions.answer: Draw two circles using the endpoints of the segment as centres and the segment length as radius (Postulate 3). Their intersection point C forms an equilateral triangle ABC — all sides equal by the radii-equality argument and Axiom 1.
questions.solution:
Given: A line segment AB.
To construct: An equilateral triangle on AB.

Construction (using Euclid's Postulates):
Step 1: Draw a circle with centre A and radius AB. (By Postulate 3)
Step 2: Draw a circle with centre B and radius BA. (By Postulate 3)
Step 3: Let C be a point where the two circles intersect.
Step 4: Draw line segments AC and BC. (By Postulate 1)

Proof that △ABC is equilateral:
- AC = AB [radii of the circle with centre A]
- BC = BA = AB [radii of the circle with centre B]

By Euclid's Axiom 1: 'Things equal to the same thing are equal to one another.'
Since AC = AB and BC = AB (both equal to AB):
  AC = BC = AB

Therefore, △ABC has all three sides equal → it is an equilateral triangle.

(Note: Euclid assumed without stating that the two circles will intersect — this is a gap in his original proof.)

questions.question: Why is Euclid's Axiom 5 ('The whole is greater than the part') considered a universal truth? Give two examples from geometry.
questions.answer: It expresses a self-evident truth about magnitudes: any whole contains its parts and more. In geometry: a line segment is longer than any of its sub-segments; an angle is greater than any of its sub-angles.
questions.solution:
Euclid's Axiom 5 states: 'The whole is greater than the part.'

Why it is universal: If B is a part of A, then A = B + C for some positive C ≠ 0. So A > B. This is self-evidently true for any additive magnitude.

Geometrical Examples:

Example 1 (Line segments):
If B lies between A and C on a line segment AC:
  AC = AB + BC, so AC > AB and AC > BC.
  The full segment AC is greater than either of its parts.

Example 2 (Angles):
If ray OB lies inside angle AOC, then:
  ∠AOC = ∠AOB + ∠BOC, so ∠AOC > ∠AOB.
  The full angle is greater than any sub-angle within it.

This axiom is the geometric basis for all inequality arguments involving parts and wholes.

questions.question: In the figure, three points A, B, C, D are collinear in that order. Given AB = CD, prove that AC = BD.
questions.answer: Add BC to both sides of AB = CD (Axiom 2: equals added to equals are equal). AB + BC = CD + BC → AC = BD.
questions.solution:
Given: A, B, C, D are collinear in that order. AB = CD.
To prove: AC = BD.

Proof:
Since B lies between A and C:
  AC = AB + BC  … (1)

Since C lies between B and D:
  BD = BC + CD  … (2)

Given: AB = CD  … (3)

Adding BC to both sides of (3):
  AB + BC = CD + BC

By Euclid's Axiom 2: 'If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.'
Since we added equals (BC = BC) to equals (AB = CD):
  AB + BC = BC + CD

From (1) and (2):
  AC = BD  ✓

Conclusion: AC = BD, proved using Euclid's Axiom 2.

questions.question: State Euclid's fifth postulate. Explain in your own words what it means geometrically. Why is it considered more complex than the other four postulates?
questions.answer: P5: If a transversal crosses two lines making co-interior angles (on one side) summing to less than 180°, those two lines meet on that side. It is complex because it involves a condition (angles < 180°) and a conclusion (lines meet) — unlike the other postulates which are simple constructions or existence statements.
questions.solution:
Euclid's Postulate 5:
'If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side of it taken together less than two right angles, then the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which the sum of angles is less than two right angles.'

Geometric meaning:
Imagine a line (transversal) crossing two other lines. On one side of the transversal, measure the two interior angles. If their sum < 180°, the two lines are not parallel — they will eventually meet (intersect) on that side. If the sum = 180°, the lines are parallel and never meet.

Why it is more complex:
1. Unlike P1–P4 (simple, brief, self-evident statements), P5 involves a conditional — a 'if... then...' structure with geometric reasoning.
2. It talks about where lines meet when extended 'indefinitely' — an infinite consideration.
3. Mathematicians tried for centuries to prove P5 from P1–P4 (thinking it was a theorem, not a postulate). They failed — and this led to the discovery of non-Euclidean geometries (where P5 does not hold), confirming it cannot be proved from the other postulates and is truly independent.

questions.question: Given: Angle ∠PQR = ∠PRQ. Point S is the midpoint of QR. Prove that ∠PQS = ∠PRS.
questions.answer: ∠PQR = ∠PRQ (given). ∠PQS and ∠PRS are halves of equal angles (since S is the midpoint of QR on a straight line — the base). By Axiom 7, halves of equals are equal, so ∠PQS = ∠PRS.
questions.solution:
Given: ∠PQR = ∠PRQ, S is the midpoint of QR.
To prove: ∠PQS = ∠PRS.

Note: Since S is the midpoint of QR, QS = SR.

In triangles PQS and PRS:
- PQ is common (no — this is about angles, not triangles directly).

Alternate approach using Euclid's axioms:

Since ∠PQR = ∠PRQ (given)  … (1)

∠PQS is part of ∠PQR, and ∠PRS is part of ∠PRQ.

Since S is the midpoint of QR, by the symmetry of the isosceles triangle PQR (given ∠PQR = ∠PRQ, triangle PQR is isosceles with PQ = PR):
  QS = SR (S is the midpoint)
  ∠PQS and ∠PRS are the angles in congruent triangles PQS and PRS
  (PQ = PR, QS = RS, PS common → triangles are congruent)

Therefore ∠PQS = ∠PRS.

Alternatively using Euclid's Axiom 7: 'Things which are halves of the same things are equal to one another.'
If ∠PQR = ∠PRQ and PS bisects the base QR symmetrically:
  ∠PQS = ½ × (full angle at Q towards S) = ∠PRS
Halves of equal angles are equal (Axiom 7). ✓

questions.question: Two lines AB and CD intersect at point O. Euclid's Theorem 5.1 proves they cannot intersect at more than one point. Now suppose three lines l, m, n are such that l and m meet at P, and m and n meet at Q. Does it follow that l and n must also meet? Explain your reasoning using Euclid's axioms/postulates.
questions.answer: Not necessarily. l and n may be parallel (never meeting) or they may meet at a third point R. The given conditions do not force l and n to intersect — parallel lines are consistent with Euclid's postulates P1–P4.
questions.solution:
Analysis:

Given:
- Lines l and m meet at point P.
- Lines m and n meet at point Q (Q ≠ P since l, m, n are distinct lines and P is on l and m, Q is on m and n).

Question: Must l and n intersect?

Reasoning:
From Theorem 5.1, we know any two distinct lines share at most one point. But this tells us nothing about whether l and n must intersect at all.

Case 1 — l and n are parallel:
Two lines can coexist without ever intersecting (parallel lines). Euclid's postulates P1–P4 are consistent with parallel lines existing (this is essentially what the 5th postulate addresses). So l and n may never meet.

Case 2 — l and n meet at some point R:
If l and n are not parallel, they will meet at exactly one point R (by Theorem 5.1), where R ≠ P and R ≠ Q.

Conclusion:
The fact that l meets m and m meets n does not force l to meet n. Transitivity of intersection does not hold in Euclidean geometry. The behaviour of l and n depends on their relative orientations, which is governed by Postulate 5 (the parallel postulate).

questions.question: Euclid's definitions define a point as 'that which has no part', a line as 'breadthless length', and a surface as 'that which has length and breadth only'. Why are these definitions considered inadequate, and why do mathematicians now treat these as undefined terms?
questions.answer: The definitions use terms ('part', 'breadth', 'length', 'evenly') that themselves need defining, creating an infinite regress. To avoid circularity, point, line, and plane are left undefined — understood intuitively and through physical models.
questions.solution:
Problem with Euclid's definitions:

1. 'A point is that which has no part.'
   - 'Part' needs to be defined. If 'part' means 'something that occupies area', then 'area' needs to be defined — and so on. This creates an infinite chain of definitions.

2. 'A line is breadthless length.'
   - 'Breadth' and 'length' are themselves geometric quantities that need defining before we can use them in a definition.

3. 'A surface is that which has length and breadth only.'
   - Same problem — 'length' and 'breadth' are assumed to be understood.

Conclusion — Why treat them as undefined:
In any logical system, there must exist some primitive, foundational terms that are not defined in terms of other things — otherwise every definition requires a prior definition in an endless regress (circular reasoning).

Mathematicians resolved this by:
- Accepting point, line, and plane as undefined terms.
- Understanding them intuitively: a point is a location with no size; a line is perfectly straight and infinitely thin; a plane is a perfectly flat surface.
- Using physical models (dots, wire edges, flat tables) to represent them without formally defining them.

This is standard in modern axiomatic systems — undefined terms are the unavoidable foundation.
